title: Open, Save, Quit
subtitle: Launch Neovim, write your work, and get out — three ways each
summary: Open a file with `nvim`. Write with `:w`. Quit with `:q`. Combine with `:wq`, `:x`, or {key:Z}{key:Z}. Discard with `:q!` or {key:Z}{key:Q}.

blocks.text: The single most-googled question about Vim is "how do I exit?" Let's get that out of the way first, then make it a real skill.
If the file exists, you see its contents. If it doesn't, you see an empty buffer that *will* be saved as `hello.txt` the first time you write. Vim doesn't create the file on disk until you save.

blocks.label: Write the buffer to its file

blocks.text: `:w` "writes" — Vim's word for save. `:w` {filename} writes to a different filename (Save As). `:w!` forces a write through read-only flags.
`:q` quits — *if* the buffer is clean. If there are unsaved changes, Vim refuses and tells you to save or force-quit. Force-quit with `:q!` discards changes and exits.

rows: Write, then quit. Always writes (even if nothing changed).
Write *if changed*, then quit. Quieter than `:wq`.
Same as `:x` — write-if-changed, then quit. Normal-mode shortcut.

blocks.text: Use {key:Z}{key:Z} as your default "done" key — no colon, no {key:Enter}, just two capital Zs. It's also the most ergonomic of the bunch.

If you ever quit with `:q!` and regret it, remember: Vim writes a per-session swapfile. The next time you open the file, Vim may offer to recover. The undo *file* (if you have `undofile` set) survives quit, so you can even reopen and undo back to a state before the changes you discarded.

blocks.title: Buffers, Files, and What `:w` Actually Does
blocks.text:
When you open a file, Vim copies it into memory as a *buffer* — an in-memory list of lines. Your edits change the buffer, not the file. `:w` (write) flushes the buffer to disk; until then, the on-disk file is unchanged.

Vim also creates a *swapfile* (`.<filename>.swp`) next to the buffer's file. The swapfile is a periodic snapshot of unsaved changes; if Vim or your machine crashes, the swapfile lets the next `nvim <file>` invocation recover. This is also what causes the "swap file already exists" prompt — usually Vim was killed without exiting cleanly, and the swap is still on disk.

The *undofile* (separate from the swap, controlled by `undofile`) persists the undo *tree* across sessions: open a file tomorrow and {key:u} can still walk back through today's edits.

Quitting with {key:Z}{key:Z} (or `:x`) writes the buffer if it's modified and exits. Quitting with {key:Z}{key:Q} (or `:q!`) deletes the swapfile and exits without writing — your unsaved buffer changes are gone, but the on-disk file is intact (it never got written this session).

blocks.title: Multiple Files
blocks.text:
Vim can hold many buffers at once. `:e` {file} opens *another* file in a new buffer, leaving the previous one in memory. `:ls` lists all loaded buffers. `:b` {n} switches to buffer N.

This matters for quitting: `:q` closes the current *window*; if other windows or buffers are open, Vim is still running. `:qa` (quit-all) closes everything. `:wqa` writes-all-then-quits-all. If you've ever pressed `:q` expecting Vim to exit and gotten "E37: No write since last change" or seen it switch to another buffer instead — it's because the buffer/window distinction matters even at this level.

Full treatment in `windows.buffers`.

blocks.title: Why It's Spelled `:w`, `:q`, `:wq`
blocks.text:
The colon-prefixed commands are not Vim's invention. They are *ex* commands — Bill Joy's line editor from 1976 — that `vi` inherited and that Vim/Neovim still implement faithfully. Ex itself was an extension of *ed*, Ken Thompson's 1969 line editor.

In ex, you addressed lines with line numbers and ran commands on them: `1,$s/foo/bar/g` ("on lines 1 to last, substitute foo with bar globally"). The single-letter command names — `w` for write, `q` for quit, `s` for substitute, `d` for delete, `y` for yank — were chosen for terseness, because users were typing on slow serial lines where every character cost real time.

This is why colon commands feel like a different language inside Vim: they *are* a different language, descended from a different lineage. The {key::} keystroke is a gear-shift. To the left of the colon you are speaking visual-mode `vi`; to the right, you are speaking ex. Both languages compose: `:w` writes, `:5,10w` writes lines 5–10 to a file, `:%s/foo/bar/g` runs an ex substitution on the whole buffer.

blocks.title: Buffers carry a dirty bit
blocks.text: Saving and quitting aren't symmetric. The buffer carries a `modified` flag that flips on the first edit and clears on a successful write. `:q` consults that flag and refuses to exit (E37) unless you force it with {key:!}; `:x` is a conditional `write-then-quit` that skips the write when the flag is already clear. {key:Z}{key:Z} is the same conditional path as `:x`; {key:Z}{key:Q} is the unconditional discard path of `:q!`. Internally these are four code paths, not four shortcuts.
